query: clinical features of femoropopliteal artery stenosis including exercise-induced symptoms and neurological manifestations

retrieved_docs.title: Peripheral Arterial Disease -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The most characteristic symptom of PAD is claudication which is a pain in the lower extremity muscles brought on by walking and relieved with rest. Although claudication has traditionally been described as cramping pain, some patients report leg fatigue, weakness, pressure, or aching. Symptoms during walking occur in the muscle group one level distal to the artery narrowed or blocked by PAD. Patients with aortoiliac artery occlusive disease, therefore, have symptoms in the thigh and buttock muscles while patients with femoropopliteal PAD have symptoms in their calf muscles. The walking distance at which symptoms occur depends on multiple factors including disease severity, walking pace, terrain, and incline.

retrieved_docs.title: Popliteal Artery Entrapment Syndrome -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: The rare clinical manifestation of Popliteal Artery Entrapment Syndrome (PAES) was defined approximately 40 years ago. It has a preference for young to middle-aged patients. The hallmark of this disease is a vascular compromise within the popliteal fossa resulting in an insidious, progressive course of exercise intolerance and calf pain with exertion relieved by rest (claudication). While the incidence of this disease has risen slightly in recent years, the true prevalence is unknown as the number of cases is likely underreported. This activity describes the evaluation, and treatment of PAES, and reviews the role of the interprofessional team in managing patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Leriche Syndrome(Archived) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Patients commonly present with claudication, which is cramping in the lower extremities (hips, thighs, buttocks) reproducible by exercise. A detailed history is essential in determining symptoms' location, severity, and duration. While impotence and sexual dysfunction may occur in the majority of patients, the hallmark of Leriche syndrome is reduced or absent femoral pulses. [2] However, due to collateral vasculature, limb-threatening ischemia is not universal. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Intermittent Claudication -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnosis of intermittent claudication include: Sciatica Atheroembolic disease Venous insufficiency Vasculitis

retrieved_docs.title: Fibromuscular Dysplasia -- History and Physical -- Lower Limb FMD
retrieved_docs.content: Claudication Cold legs Ischemic feet

retrieved_docs.title: Aortoiliac Occlusive Disease -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Obtaining a medical history and performing a physical examination are critical in assessing disease severity and guiding management. Patients commonly present with cramping pain in the lower extremities that occurs during and after physical activity and is relieved by rest. This classic symptom, claudication, is caused by reduced muscle blood flow due to arterial stenosis. The location of the muscle cramping can help determine the severity and extent of the stenosis. More proximal symptoms, such as buttock or thigh claudication, typically suggest more significant or higher-level occlusions, often involving the aortoiliac region.

retrieved_docs.title: Intermittent Claudication -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The typical presentation of intermittent claudication is lower extremity pain during ambulation that is relieved with rest. The progression of symptoms is gradual. The pain may be localized to the buttocks or the lower leg, depending on the site of occlusion. Patients with aortoiliac disease frequently develop buttock pain.

retrieved_docs.title: Intermittent Claudication -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Intermittent claudication (IC) typically refers to lower extremity skeletal muscle pain that occurs during exercise. IC presents when there is insufficient oxygen delivery to meet the metabolic requirements of the skeletal muscles. IC is a common manifestation of peripheral arterial disease (PAD), which includes atherosclerotic stenosis of arteries in the extremities. IC is commonly localized to the thigh, hip, buttock, and calf muscles. Pain within these muscle groups is reproducibly induced by walking and relieved with rest. This activity reviews the cause, pathophysiology, and presentation of intermittent claudication and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in the management of these patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Intermittent Claudication -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Intermittent claudication (IC) typically refers to lower extremity skeletal muscle pain that occurs during exercise. IC presents when there is insufficient oxygen delivery to meet the metabolic requirements of the skeletal muscles. Intermittent claudication is a common manifestation of peripheral arterial disease (PAD), which includes atherosclerotic stenosis of arteries in the extremities. IC is commonly localized to the thigh, hip, buttock, and calf muscles. Pain within these muscle groups is reproducibly induced by walking and relieved with rest. The severity of pain can sometimes correlate with the degree of stenosis or blockage in arteries supplying the lower extremities. [1] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Leriche Syndrome(Archived) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical treatment options for Leriche syndrome include thromboendarterectomy, aortobifemoral bypass (AFB), and percutaneous transluminal angioplasty (PTA) with or without stenting. [7] [3] [8] AFB is preferred over TEA, mainly when intervention is necessary for arteries deep in the pelvis. Long-term patency of surgically repaired vessels in AFB has been recognized as superior to thromboendarterectomy and PTA with or without stenting in surgically fit patients. The long-term patency rates of AFB approach 85-90% at 5 years and 75-80% at 10 years. [3] PTA and stenting are the interventions of choice in patients with multiple comorbidities, particularly those resulting in reduced lung function. Medical management includes smoking cessation, management of diabetes, antiplatelet, and statin therapy, in addition to antihypertensive therapy. Implementation of a walking exercise program has been shown to improve walking ability by 50% - 200%. The exercise program should consist of daily 30-minute walking sessions. Intolerable claudication pain should be the endpoint for each session. [9] Smoking cessation and lowering glucose and lipid levels (LDL <100 mg/dL and HbA1c below 7%) are primary objectives of medical management. Cilostazol, a phosphodiesterase III inhibitor, may be administered to treat the patient's claudication symptoms. Cilostazol may also have benefits in graft patency and prevention of stenosis. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Intermittent Claudication -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Physical examination of these patients may show evidence of arterial insufficiency. The affected limb may feel cool and have diminished pulses. The physical examination should include an assessment of femoral, popliteal, dorsalis pedis, and posterior tibial artery pulses. The patient’s presentation should be carefully distinguished from other causes of leg pain like neurogenic pseudoclaudication (spinal stenosis), musculoskeletal pain, and venous claudication associated with leg swelling and varicosities. In general, the presence of strong pedal pulses argues against a diagnosis of intermittent claudication.

retrieved_docs.title: Percutaneous Transluminal Angioplasty and Balloon Catheters -- Indications -- Peripheral Vascular Disease
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with disabling symptoms, despite lifestyle adjustment, supervised exercise programs, and pharmacotherapy, are candidates for revascularization. The choice of revascularization procedure depends on symptomatology, patient risk, location and extent of disease, comorbidities, patient's functional status, and patient preference. Angioplasty with stenting is the preferred procedure except in cases of - Eccentric calcified stenosis, a long segment of stenosis, or multifocal stenosis. [15] [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Intermittent Claudication -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: The complications of intermittent claudication include: Lack of exercise endurance Prolonged wound healing Impotence Blue toe syndrome Ischemic leg

retrieved_docs.title: Leriche Syndrome(Archived) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: AIOD is classified as Type I when confined to the distal abdominal aorta and common iliac arteries, Type II when predominately distal abdominal aorta with disease extension into common iliac and external iliac arteries, and Type III when affecting the aortoiliac segment and femoropopliteal vessels. [2] AIOD, when symptomatic, classically presents with a triad of claudication, impotence, and absence of femoral pulses. Claudication refers to cramping leg pain reproducible with exercise.

retrieved_docs.title: Popliteal Artery Entrapment Syndrome -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: A high degree of clinical suspicion is warranted as the patients affected with the PAES can face significant physical impairment. Symptoms usually present in the second to third decade of life and are insidious in onset [5] . Classically, the affected patients are younger athletes without a previous history of cardiovascular risk factors [20] [7] . Typically, patients present with the complaints of leg and foot claudication that can be quite severe and disabling [7] [10] [20] . Besides, the patient might likewise notice lower extremity numbness, paresthesia, discoloration, pallor, and coolness. [20] [12] Moreover, the physical examinations usually reveal hypertrophy of the calf muscles [20] [13] [21] . Also, the dorsiflexion/plantar flexion of the foot may elicit diminished, unequal, or even absent pulses [20] [22] , and as the disease progress, the ankle-brachial index (ABI) will also tilt towards the lower limit [20] [23] . Tercan et al . in a study also reported that the absence of the food pulses during the active plantar flexion and passive dorsiflexion of the foot is characteristic for the PAES [24] .

retrieved_docs.title: Intermittent Claudication -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Any patient with intermittent claudication or peripheral arterial disease should also be evaluated for coexisting cardiac disease. If symptoms are worsening despite medical treatment, or if symptoms are considered disabling or lifestyle-limiting, intervention may be considered. Other considerations for invasive procedures may include atherosclerotic lesion characteristics and patient prognosis. Intervention may initially involve minimally-invasive endovascular angioplasty, stenting, or atherectomy.

retrieved_docs.content: procedures, most commonly performed via the femoral artery; inspection of the femoral artery may reveal a thigh bruit indicating turbulent flow. Histories of peripheral vascular bypass or below‑knee amputation are often followed by physical‑therapy regimens. In cardiac taxonomy, “other and ill‑defined heart disease” is synonymous with vascular dilation of the heart and includes myocardial disease (SMQ), which itself contains conditions such as bacterial myocarditis. Inflammatory cardiac conditions like pericarditis are clinically associated with abnormal electrocardiogram patterns. Together, atherosclerosis, its inflammatory forms, related lipid‑metabolism disorders, metabolic disturbances, vascular surgical interventions, and targeted pharmacologic therapies constitute an interrelated network of cardiovascular pathology aimed at reducing vascular complications and improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 829
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal aortic atherosclerosis is identified as a pathological finding within the aorta abdominalis, indicating that atherosclerotic disease commonly involves the abdominal segment of the aorta. The aorta abdominalis itself is linked to peripheral vascular bypass, reflecting that surgical bypass procedures are often considered for restoring blood flow when significant aortic disease is present. In the lower extremity, the arteria femoralis (femoral artery) may present with لغط فخذي (thigh bruit), a clinical sign that suggests turbulent flow or stenosis in the femoral vessels. The femoral artery is also a direct procedural site for peripheral vascular bypass, demonstrating that bypass surgery can be performed on the femoral artery to circumvent occlusive lesions. Documentation of peripheral vascular bypass is associated with a surgical history that is relevant to physical‑therapy treatment, encompassing details such as the type of surgery, timing, and patient identifiers. Together, these relationships illustrate how atherosclerotic disease of the abdominal aorta and femoral artery can lead to clinical findings (e.g., thigh bruit) and necessitate peripheral vascular bypass surgery, with the procedure’s history being integral to multidisciplinary patient management, including physical therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2116
retrieved_docs.content: The arteria femoralis (femoral artery) is clinically associated with the finding site described as لغط فخذي, which corresponds to a thigh bruit. Detection of a bruit over the thigh indicates turbulent blood flow within the femoral artery and can be a useful physical examination sign for evaluating vascular conditions such as arterial stenosis, aneurysm, or peripheral arterial disease in the lower extremity. Recognizing this relationship aids clinicians in localizing vascular abnormalities and guiding further diagnostic or therapeutic interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 1 -- Community 169
retrieved_docs.content: for peripheral vascular bypass procedures performed on the femoral artery (arteria femoralis), where turbulent flow may be detected as a thigh bruit (لغط فخذي). Such vascular interventions are often documented alongside postoperative physical therapy and may culminate in procedures such as below‑knee amputation. Myocarditis (الْتِهابُ التَّأَمُوْر) is another cardiovascular condition that commonly presents with abnormal electrocardiographic findings, underscoring the interconnected nature of inflammatory, metabolic, and atherosclerotic processes in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 200
retrieved_docs.content: Arteriografie functions as the overarching category for imaging of the femoral and lower‑extremity arterial tree, encompassing the contrast arteriogram of the femoral and lower extremity arteries and its inversely defined sub‑type, the contrast aortogram. Digital subtraction angiography (DSA) of the aorta and lower limb is linked to both the femoral‑lower‑extremity contrast arteriogram and the contrast aortogram; DSA protocols with 4–6, 7–9, ≤3, or ≥10 acquisition runs are classified as “other” for the femoral‑lower‑extremity study but as “primary” for the contrast aortogram, with unilateral ≤3‑run studies also mapped to the Arabic descriptor “صورة الأبهر” (aortic image). The femoral artery (arteria femoralis) is explicitly associated with the femoral‑lower‑extremity contrast arteriogram, underscoring its central role in peripheral vascular imaging. The abdominal aorta (aorta abdominalis) is directly related to peripheral vascular bypass surgery, in which the femoral artery serves as the operative site for creating the bypass to restore flow in compromised peripheral vessels. Clinical examination of the femoral artery may reveal a thigh bruit (لغط فخذي), a sign of turbulent flow suggestive of stenosis or occlusion that often leads to consideration of revascularization. Documentation of the peripheral vascular bypass appears in the patient’s surgical history and is linked to subsequent physical‑therapy‑related care, specifying the procedure type and timing within the overall treatment plan. Together, these entities and relationships define a structured framework for categorizing aortic and lower‑extremity angiographic studies, guiding protocol selection, accurate reporting, and multidisciplinary management of peripheral arterial disease.

Surgical history includes peripheral vascular bypass procedures performed on the abdominal aorta (aorta abdominalis) with the femoral artery (arteria femoralis) as the direct site, often documented alongside physical‑therapy‑related surgery and below‑knee amputation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2328
retrieved_docs.content: The femoral artery (arteria femoralis) is identified as the anatomical location where a thigh bruit (لغط فخذي) may be auscultated, indicating abnormal vascular flow in the thigh region. This same artery serves as the direct operative site for a peripheral vascular bypass, a surgical procedure performed to restore adequate blood flow distal to occlusive disease. In the patient’s medical record, the peripheral vascular bypass is recorded as part of the surgical history that is linked to subsequent physical‑therapy treatment, with specific information captured about the type of surgery and its timing. Together, these relationships underscore the importance of recognizing femoral artery findings, accurately documenting bypass interventions, and integrating surgical history with rehabilitative care to optimize patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1513
retrieved_docs.content: التصلب العصيدي is a vascular condition that is clinically associated with several observable signs and health considerations. It is linked to the presence of a systolic murmur (النفخة الانقباضية), indicating turbulent blood flow during cardiac contraction, and to arterial bruits such as a carotid bruit (لغط الشريان السباتي) and a femoral bruit (لغط فخذي), which reflect turbulent flow in the carotid and femoral arteries respectively. Patients with التصلب العصيدي may also present with a limp (عرج), suggesting compromised limb perfusion or musculoskeletal involvement, and the condition emphasizes the importance of routine health maintenance (حفظ الصحة الروتيني) to monitor and manage cardiovascular risk. Additionally, التصلب العصيدي is associated with ذَبْحَةُ برنزميتال, a specific clinical or pathological finding that further characterizes the disease spectrum. The condition includes a more extensive form known as التصلب العصيدي المعمم, representing a generalized manifestation of arterial hardening. Recognizing these interrelated clinical features and the broader subtype aids clinicians in comprehensive assessment, early detection, and targeted management of patients with arterial sclerosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2523
retrieved_docs.content: Arteriosclerosis (التصلب العصيدي) is clinically associated with a range of systemic and vascular manifestations. It is linked to renal dysfunction (اختلال وظيفة الكلى), indicating that impaired kidney function often co‑occurs with arterial hardening. Patients with arteriosclerosis may present with a systolic murmur (النفخة الانقباضية), reflecting turbulent blood flow across narrowed arterial segments. Tobacco use (تعاطي التَبغ) is a recognized risk factor that contributes to the development and progression of arteriosclerosis. Routine health maintenance (حفظ الصحة الروتيني) is emphasized as an important preventive strategy to mitigate the impact of arteriosclerosis on overall health. Physical findings such as a limp (عرج) can arise from compromised peripheral circulation, while auscultatory signs like a carotid bruit (لغط الشريان السباتي) and a femoral bruit (لغط فخذي) are characteristic auditory clues of arterial narrowing in the neck and thigh regions, respectively. Additionally, arteriosclerosis is associated with the entity ذَبْحَةُ برنزميتال, suggesting a possible comorbid or related condition. The disease spectrum includes a specific subtype termed generalized arteriosclerosis (التصلب العصيدي المعمم), reflecting a more extensive involvement of the arterial system. Recognizing these interrelated clinical features is essential for comprehensive assessment, risk stratification, and management of patients with arteriosclerosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 231
retrieved_docs.content: surgical removal of atherosclerotic plaque—endarterectomy of head‑and‑neck vessels (استئصال باطنة الشريان)—is indicated in the context of arterial thrombosis or embolism. The intracranial arterial structure can reveal intracerebral atherosclerotic changes, providing an indirect site for such procedures. Together, these entities illustrate the interconnected pathophysiology of precerebral arterial disease, basilar artery involvement, cerebral infarction, transient ischemic events, clinical presentation, and both medical and surgical management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2140
retrieved_docs.content: Dolní končetina, the lower limb, is identified as the anatomical site where ضعف الأطراف السفلية لـ (weakness of the lower limbs) is observed, indicating that clinical assessment of lower‑extremity weakness should focus on this region. In addition, dolní končetina is linked to التصلب العصيدي لشرايين الأطراف (arteriosclerosis of the peripheral arteries), underscoring the relationship between lower‑limb vascular disease and functional impairment. Recognizing that weakness of the lower limbs may arise in the context of peripheral arterial sclerosis emphasizes the importance of evaluating vascular health when patients present with lower‑extremity weakness, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and management.
